project.name: ruvector slice 3 — Neural Primitives (math + graph + dag + cnn)
project.description: RuVector slice 3 covers the foundational neural-primitive crates that downstream algorithm crates build on: ruvector-math (Optimal Transport, Information Geometry, Product Manifolds), ruvector-graph (distributed Neo4j-compatible hypergraph DB with SIMD), ruvector-dag (DAG structures for query plan optimization with neural learning), and ruvector-cnn (CNN feature extraction for image embeddings with SIMD acceleration). Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.summary: Univariate tropical (max-plus) polynomial with construction from coefficients or monomials, evaluation, root extraction, and linear-region counting used to characterize piecewise-linear networks.

nodes.name: TropicalMonomial
nodes.summary: Single tropical monomial (coefficient + exponent) with eval and multiplication operators in the (max,+) semiring.

nodes.name: MultivariateTropicalPolynomial
nodes.summary: Multivariate tropical polynomial with eval over n input variables and term-count metadata, supporting analysis of multi-input piecewise-linear functions.

nodes.name: roots
nodes.summary: Computes the tropical roots (knots) of a univariate tropical polynomial, identifying the breakpoints of the piecewise-linear function.

nodes.name: mul
nodes.summary: Tropical multiplication of two polynomials in the (max,+) semiring, combining monomials and applying max reduction.

nodes.name: add
nodes.summary: Tropical addition (max) of two polynomials, merging monomials by exponent and keeping the dominant coefficient per exponent.

nodes.name: eval
nodes.summary: Evaluates a multivariate tropical polynomial at a point x by taking the max over all monomial values.

nodes.name: from_coeffs
nodes.summary: Builds a univariate tropical polynomial from a coefficient vector indexed by exponent.

tour.title: Slice 3 Orientation: Four Foundational Crates
tour.description: Slice 3 establishes the neural-primitive foundation that downstream RuVector algorithms (attention, mincut, GNN) build on. These four READMEs introduce the responsibilities: ruvector-math (geometric and information-theoretic primitives), ruvector-graph (distributed Neo4j-compatible hypergraph DB), ruvector-dag (DAG structures with attention, SONA, self-healing, post-quantum crypto), and ruvector-cnn (SIMD/INT8 CNN feature extraction). Read them as the table of contents for the tour.

tour.title: Slice 3 Crate Manifests & Public Surface
tour.description: Each crate's Cargo.toml declares its feature flags (SIMD ISAs, INT8, contrastive, distributed, qudag, ...) and dependency graph, while lib.rs is the canonical entry that re-exports the public API. Together they define the compile-time configuration matrix and the runtime symbol surface you will navigate for the rest of the tour.
tour.languageLesson: Rust crates use Cargo features (e.g. `simd-avx2`, `int8`, `distributed`) to gate `#[cfg(feature = "x")]` code paths so a single source tree compiles into many runtime profiles.

tour.title: Slice 3 Math I: Information Geometry & Manifolds
tour.description: Information geometry treats probability distributions as a Riemannian manifold; Fisher information defines the metric tensor, and natural gradients descend along that geometry rather than naive Euclidean directions. These files implement the Fisher matrix, K-FAC approximation, the natural-gradient step, and the product-manifold abstraction that lets later code mix spherical/hyperbolic/Euclidean factors in one operation.
tour.languageLesson: Rust generics with `T: Float` bounds let one Fisher implementation specialize to `f32` (SIMD-friendly) or `f64` (precision) without code duplication.

tour.title: Slice 3 Math II: Optimal Transport, Spectral & Persistent Homology
tour.description: Optimal transport gives a principled distance between distributions; Sinkhorn entropically regularizes the problem so it becomes a fixed-point iteration on a matrix-scaling, and sliced Wasserstein avoids the quadratic blow-up by 1D projection. Pair these with Chebyshev polynomial filters and persistent homology to get the spectral + topological toolkit consumed by GNN and clustering code later.

tour.title: Slice 3 Math III: Tensor Networks
tour.description: Tensor networks factor high-dimensional arrays into chains/trees of small cores, exposing low-rank structure that dense linear algebra would miss. CP, Tucker, and Tensor-Train decompositions provide three trade-off points (rank vs. flexibility vs. memory) and are the substrate for compressed neural representations used by slice 2 attention/embedding code.

tour.title: Slice 3 Graph: Storage, Transactions & Sharding
tour.description: The hypergraph DB layer mirrors Neo4j's property-graph model but adds true hyperedges (relations on more than two nodes). graph.rs assembles the storage primitives (nodes, hyperedges, transactions) into a coherent store; the distributed/ tree (shard, coordinator) then federates that store horizontally so the same Cypher API addresses a sharded cluster transparently.
tour.languageLesson: Rust's `RwLock` and `Arc` give shared-read/exclusive-write semantics without a GC; transactions wrap the same primitives in a snapshot to give ACID semantics on top of a lock-free read path.

tour.title: Slice 3 Graph: Cypher Pipeline (Parse to JIT to Operators)
tour.description: A Cypher string travels through parser -> semantic analyzer -> optimizer -> physical plan -> JIT-compiled kernel -> physical operators (NodeScan/EdgeScan/Filter/Join/...) before touching storage. Each stage is its own module so you can intercept at any level. ARCHITECTURE.md maps the full pipeline; query_jit.rs is the surprise — hot plans are lowered to executable code for column-stride performance.

tour.title: Slice 3 Graph: Hybrid AI Surface (GNN + RAG + Semantic Search)
tour.description: On top of the operators from Step 7, the hybrid layer composes GNNs, vector indexes, semantic search, and RAG so a single Cypher query can mix graph traversal with vector retrieval. This is the key differentiator versus stock Neo4j and the bridge into the DAG/attention work in later steps.

tour.title: Slice 3 DAG: Core Structures & Mincut Engines
tour.description: QueryDag is the in-memory plan representation that the DAG crate operates on: operator nodes carry kernel metadata, traversal iterators expose topological/DFS/BFS orderings, and the mincut engine partitions the DAG to find optimal cut points (used later by mincut-gated attention). This is the data structure that all subsequent DAG-layer features manipulate.
tour.languageLesson: Rust uses petgraph-style index-based graphs (`NodeIndex`/`EdgeIndex`) instead of pointer-based ones — this side-steps borrow-checker conflicts when an operation needs to read and mutate the graph simultaneously.

tour.title: Slice 3 DAG: Attention Mechanisms
tour.description: The DagAttention trait abstracts multiple attention variants over the DAG: topological (forward-only), causal-cone (history-bounded), hierarchical Lorentz (curved-space across DAG depth), and mincut-gated (uses Step 9's mincut to throttle cross-cluster attention). The IMPLEMENTATION_NOTES doc explains the trade-offs between them — read it first, then skim each variant.
tour.languageLesson: A trait-based attention dispatch (`Box<dyn DagAttention>`) keeps each variant in its own module while letting the executor pick at runtime — the Rust equivalent of a strategy class hierarchy in OO languages.

tour.title: Slice 3 DAG: SONA Continual Learning & Self-Healing
tour.description: SONA (Self-Organizing Neural Architecture) is the cognitive loop: a reasoning bank stores task-conditioned trajectories, EWC++ penalizes weights drifting from prior tasks (preventing catastrophic forgetting), and MicroLoRA injects task-specific low-rank deltas. Paired with it, the healing orchestrator runs anomaly + drift detection and dispatches repair strategies — SONA improves the model, healing keeps the runtime sane.

tour.title: Slice 3 DAG: Post-Quantum QuDAG
tour.description: QuDAG hardens the DAG for a post-quantum future: ML-KEM (lattice-based KEM) for key exchange, ML-DSA (Dilithium) for signatures, identities wrap those primitives, and the network layer carries the resulting authenticated traffic. Tokens/rewards bolt staking on top, making QuDAG a distributed-incentive substrate as well as a crypto layer.

tour.title: Slice 3 CNN: Backbone, Layers & SIMD Kernels
tour.description: ruvector-cnn delivers compact feature extraction. The MobileNetV3 backbone composes layer primitives (Conv2D, batchnorm, linear, pooling) into an efficient inverted-residual stack. The simd/ tree then ships per-ISA implementations of the same kernel — AVX2/AVX-512 for x86, NEON for ARM, WASM SIMD for the browser, plus a scalar reference path and Winograd F(2,3) transforms — dispatched at runtime via CPU feature detection.
tour.languageLesson: Rust's `std::arch` exposes SIMD intrinsics behind `#[target_feature(enable = "avx2")]` and `unsafe fn`. A safe outer wrapper does runtime CPU detection once and then dispatches into the appropriate intrinsic kernel — keeping `unsafe` contained while still going fast. Const generics on layer types let the compiler monomorphize a 3x3 conv into a fully unrolled loop.

tour.title: Slice 3 CNN: INT8 Quantization Pipeline
tour.description: Quantization replaces fp32 weights with int8 plus per-tensor scales and zero-points. params.rs defines the representation; calibration.rs computes scales from a calibration dataset; graph_rewrite.rs lowers the fp32 graph from Step 13 into INT8 ops; int8/kernels/simd.rs is the matching SIMD kernel. tests/kernel_equivalence.rs proves the INT8 path stays within tolerance of fp32.

tour.title: Slice 3 Capstone: Contrastive Training & Exotic Examples
tour.description: The contrastive module (InfoNCE, triplet loss, augmentation) trains the CNN embeddings the rest of RuVector consumes. The exotic examples (synthetic reflex organism, living simulation, self-healing demo) compose every primitive from this tour — math manifolds, hypergraph storage, DAG attention, SONA learning, healing, and CNN features — into runnable scenarios. These are the integration tests for the entire neural-primitive stack and the on-ramp into slices 1 and 2.
